column23:
I went to a play at the Arvada Center and am super impressed with all that they offer. If you have not already it would be worth a field trip.  The place should feel comfortable and cozy, not like a library.  There should be an open to the public area and thoughtful staff on site.  As the owner of Connects Workspace having a "community builder" will be important. 
I grew up in Golden and we used to go to dances in what is now the library.  I know longer have teens in the house, but I think this is a HUGE need in Golden. As safe place that kids can have fun and be loud but are safe.

column9: I’d love to have a family friendly space for the creative arts that is more affordable/ accessible than foothills. Miners has classes for kids or for adults but not both, similar to rh library and foothills. I am a full time worker with two small children so the time I have free in evenings or weekends I like to spend with my kids. I’d like to have some indoor options that we can do TOGETHER with other families. Like combined art classes.

column23: We have great spaces but I’d like to have more opportunities to do social activities with all ages, including my kids. We don’t have nearby grandparents so my ideal space would have lots of classes and programs where ages 0-110 can do things TOGETHER. I would like to have friends who are my parents age or older and I’d like my kids to have friends that age too! I don’t see any reason we could attend a photography ,dance, cooking, or pottery class that is accessible to 3 year olds, 30 year olds, 60 year olds, and 90 year olds.

column9: It's hard to say. This might be a Colorado thing, but I find that the culture here in the Front Range is less open than where I came from (Austin TX). My theory is that Austin didn't have the big beautiful landscape to recreate in, so community events have been the central focus, and people tend to prioritize socialization over being out in nature. People are very friendly here, but I've tried to throw gatherings and everyone's always skiing or camping or "out".  I thought about starting a supper club, but living on Lookout Mtn is not a great location for that (Denver, better). I'd consider moving to town but its crazy expensive for what you get. So, I'm still trying to figure it out. I've joined groups, play a lot of pickleball and belong to a club, but so far none of those have yielded friends - just things to do. It's generally hard to move to a new place!
column10: Clubs (book, film, language, etc.), Study or work space, Ceramics, Talks or community discussions, on the Talks above - I love salons! culture, arts, music. I'm a former film professor and I would totally start a film club if there was a space to show films, too.

column23: I would like a makerspace that is complimentary with Clear Creek Makerspace, as in having different capabilities. It might be interesting to have some kind of relationships between a makerspace, the Foothills Art Center, and Miner's Playhouse.

column9: It takes time to connect with people.  I do a lot of one-off things at various places in Golden, but I'm not seeing the same people over and over again, so I'm not getting a chance to learn their stories and tell them my story.  I see myself being involved enough at a makers space through participation and volunteering to start being around the same people enough to start building deeper connections.
column10: Household repair clinics (fixing small appliances, bikes, gear, etc.), Study or work space, Electronics / Robots, 3D Printing / Design, Sewing, Mending, or Fiber Arts, Outdoor skills or environmental workshops,
column11: Metalworking (Welding), 3D Printing / Design, Fixing things instead of throwing them away
column13:
I don't think I have any issues feeling welcome in places like the library, rec center, Miners Alley Playhouse, Golden History Museum, etc.  I think it's the lack of opportunity to connect that's missing (for me), because:
1. I don't have a reason to frequent those places (which I guess boils down to programming style, maybe?)
2. When I am at those places, I don't have a reason, or an opening to talk with people (which I guess also boils down to programming style as well?)

Location is important as well.  I'd like to spend time at Clear Creek Makerspace, but even 15 minutes away is too far for me to do more than a couple of times per month.
column20: Paying for individual classes or workshops, Becoming a low-cost monthly member, Volunteering ocassionally, Teaching or helping lead activities, Helping with events, Donating tools, supplies, or equipment
column21: Evening and weekend hours, Ability to work from there.  As a remote employee, if I could work, then take a break to tinker with something throughout the day, that would be amazing.
column23:
Conceptually, I think it would look a lot like Clear Creek Makerspace.  It would be open 24/7 to paying members, and it would provide affordable and/or free programming to non-members.  It would be ran by a strong group of core volunteers and frequent visitors that loved sharing things they are passionate about with insatiably curious people like me.  It would attract a diverse group of people of all ages who want to learn, create, and share ideas together.

The actual programs and amenities offered don't need to match Clear Creek Makerspace exactly, in fact, it might be better if it complimented CCM, rather than duplicated CCM, but the overall model of CCM appears to be financially sustainable and popular, which would go a long way toward brining something like this into existence and keeping it around for a long time.
